title: Viral diseases — measles, HIV, tobacco mosaic virus
examTip: AQA picks these three viral diseases specifically because they test different ideas: vaccination (measles), immune system damage (HIV), and plant disease with a knock-on effect on photosynthesis (TMV). Know each one's spread, symptoms, and control method.
introTips: You only need three viral diseases for AQA: measles, HIV, and tobacco mosaic virus. They each appear in different types of exam question, so it's worth knowing what makes each one distinct.
The biggest trap students fall into is writing 'antibiotics' as a treatment for viral diseases. Antibiotics don't work on viruses — this lesson will make that impossible to forget.
TMV is the odd one out — it's a plant disease. But AQA loves asking how it links to photosynthesis, so read that section carefully.
intro: Three viruses. Three very different stories. Measles spreads through the air and was killing children in the UK before vaccination changed everything. HIV attacks the very system your body uses to fight infection, leaving you vulnerable to diseases a healthy immune system would brush off. And tobacco mosaic virus doesn't infect people at all — it goes after plants, and understanding why it's so damaging comes down to photosynthesis. Learn what each one does, how it spreads, and how we control it.

keyPoints.heading: Measles — symptoms, spread, and why the MMR vaccine matters
keyPoints.content: Measles is caused by a virus spread through airborne droplets — coughing and sneezing. The symptoms are a fever and a distinctive red blotchy rash that usually starts on the face and spreads downward. It sounds mild but measles can be fatal, especially in young children and people with weakened immune systems — it can lead to serious complications including pneumonia and brain damage. The key defence is the MMR vaccine (measles, mumps, rubella), given in childhood. To protect a whole population, you need roughly 95% of people vaccinated — this is called herd immunity. When enough people are immune, the virus can't spread even to those who aren't vaccinated (such as babies too young for the vaccine). If vaccination rates drop, outbreaks return — which is exactly what has happened in parts of the world when vaccine hesitancy spreads.
writeBullets: Can be fatal — complications include pneumonia, brain damage

keyTerms.def: A viral disease spread by airborne droplets; causes fever and red rash; can be fatal; prevented by MMR vaccine
Vaccine that protects against measles, mumps, and rubella — given in childhood
When enough people in a population are immune that a disease cannot spread, protecting even unvaccinated individuals

keyPoints.memTip: Measles = Droplets, Fever + Rash, Fatal, MMR. Use the initials DFRM — Droplets, Fever-Rash, can be fatal, MMR prevents it. Say them aloud three times, then write them from memory.

keyPoints.heading: HIV — how it attacks your immune system and what AIDS means
keyPoints.content: HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus) is spread through sexual contact and the exchange of body fluids — most commonly unprotected sex or sharing needles when injecting drugs. It targets your immune system, specifically the white blood cells that coordinate your immune response. Over time, HIV destroys these cells until your immune system can no longer defend you against infections and diseases it would normally deal with easily. At that point, the person is said to have AIDS (Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome). AIDS is not caused by HIV directly — it's caused by the infections and cancers that take hold once the immune system is too damaged to fight them. HIV can be controlled with antiretroviral drugs, which stop the virus replicating and keep the immune system functional. These drugs do not cure HIV — they manage it. With treatment, someone with HIV can live a normal life span without developing AIDS.
writeBullets: HIV spread by: sexual contact, exchange of body fluids (sharing needles)
HIV attacks and destroys white blood cells (immune system)
AIDS = when immune system is too damaged to fight infections
AIDS is caused by secondary infections, not HIV directly
Antiretroviral drugs control HIV but do NOT cure it

keyTerms.def: Human Immunodeficiency Virus — destroys white blood cells, weakening the immune system
Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome — condition where the immune system is so damaged it cannot fight infections
Drugs that prevent HIV from replicating — they manage HIV but do not cure it

keyPoints.memTip: HIV destroys → immune system fails → AIDS. The chain matters. HIV is the virus; AIDS is what happens when the immune system collapses. AQA will test whether you know they're different things. Antiretroviral = controls, not cures — another common exam distinction.

keyPoints.heading: Tobacco mosaic virus (TMV) — a plant disease with photosynthesis consequences
keyPoints.content: Tobacco mosaic virus is a plant pathogen — it doesn't infect humans at all. It attacks a wide range of plants including tomatoes, peppers, and (as the name suggests) tobacco. The visible sign of infection is a mosaic pattern of discolouration on the leaves — patches of yellowed or pale green tissue mixed with normal green, giving a mottled, mosaic-like appearance. The reason this matters biologically is what's behind that discolouration: the virus damages the chloroplasts in infected cells, or prevents them forming properly. Chloroplasts are where photosynthesis happens, so damaged chloroplasts mean less photosynthesis. Less photosynthesis means less glucose produced. Less glucose means less energy and less material for growth — so infected plants are stunted and produce lower crop yields. It's a cascade: virus → chloroplast damage → reduced photosynthesis → stunted growth.
writeBullets: TMV is a plant pathogen — affects plants, NOT humans
Causes mosaic pattern of discolouration on leaves (yellow/pale patches)
Less photosynthesis → less glucose → stunted growth, reduced yield

keyTerms.def: A virus that infects plants; causes mosaic discolouration on leaves by damaging chloroplasts
A mottled pattern of yellow and green patches on plant leaves — characteristic sign of TMV infection
Reduced growth due to insufficient photosynthesis — a consequence of TMV damaging chloroplasts

keyPoints.memTip: TMV chain: Mosaic pattern → Chloroplast damage → Less photosynthesis → Stunted growth. Write those four words as a chain with arrows, then close your notes and redraw it. The chain is the answer — AQA often asks you to explain the full sequence.

keyPoints.heading: How viral diseases are controlled
keyPoints.content: Controlling viruses is harder than controlling bacterial infections, because antibiotics don't work on viruses. The main strategies are: vaccination — giving the immune system a preview of the pathogen so it can respond fast if the real thing appears (measles is a perfect example); antiviral drugs — drugs that target specific stages of a virus's replication cycle (antiretrovirals for HIV are one example), though these are harder to develop than antibiotics and often only manage rather than cure; hygiene — handwashing, safe food handling, covering coughs and sneezes reduces transmission of many viruses; and quarantine — isolating infected individuals to prevent the virus spreading to new hosts. One thing to underline: if a question asks how to treat a viral disease, do not write 'antibiotics'. That answer loses marks every time.
writeBullets: Vaccination — gives immune system a preview of the virus
Antiviral drugs — target virus replication (e.g. antiretrovirals for HIV)
Antibiotics do NOT work on viruses — never write this as a treatment

keyTerms.def: Introducing a weakened or inactive form of a pathogen to stimulate the immune system without causing disease
Drugs that interfere with virus replication — they manage viral infection rather than curing it
Isolating infected individuals to prevent a disease spreading

keyPoints.memTip: VAHQ — Vaccination, Antivirals, Hygiene, Quarantine. And one massive NOT: antibiotics do NOT treat viruses. If VAHQ sounds hard, remember it as 'Very Annoying Highly Questionable' — absurd enough to stick.

1.question: A child has not been vaccinated against measles. Measles is still rare in her school because 96% of the other children are vaccinated. What is the name for this type of protection?
1.correctFeedback: ✅ That's herd immunity — when vaccination rates are high enough, the virus can't spread even to unvaccinated individuals.
1.wrongFeedback: This is herd immunity. When enough people in a population are immune, the pathogen can't find new hosts to infect, so even unvaccinated individuals are protected. It's why vaccination rates matter for everyone, not just the person being vaccinated.

2.question: A patient with HIV is told they have developed AIDS. Which of the following best explains what AIDS means?
options: The HIV virus has spread to their lungs
The immune system is now so damaged it cannot fight off other infections
The HIV virus has been cured by antiretroviral drugs
The patient has developed a fever and rash from the HIV virus
2.correctFeedback: ✅ Exactly right. AIDS is the stage where the immune system can no longer defend against infections it would normally handle. It's the consequence of HIV destroying white blood cells over time.
2.wrongFeedback: AIDS isn't a direct symptom of HIV — it's what happens when the immune system is so badly damaged that it can't fight off other infections. HIV destroys white blood cells; once there aren't enough left, even minor infections become life-threatening. That stage is AIDS.

commonMistakes: Writing 'antibiotics' as a treatment for any viral disease — antibiotics only work on bacteria. For viruses: vaccination prevents them, antivirals manage them.
Confusing HIV and AIDS — HIV is the virus; AIDS is the syndrome that develops when the immune system is too damaged to function. They are different things.
Saying HIV is spread through 'any contact' — it requires exchange of body fluids. Casual contact (shaking hands, sharing a toilet) does not transmit HIV.
Forgetting that TMV is a plant disease — some students write that it affects humans because it's listed alongside measles and HIV. It does not infect humans.
Describing the mosaic discolouration without explaining why it matters — the mark is for linking it to chloroplast damage → reduced photosynthesis → stunted growth.
examTips: For measles questions, know the route (airborne droplets), the symptoms (fever and red rash), and the control method (MMR vaccine). Three facts, easy marks.
For HIV questions, be clear that antiretroviral drugs control but don't cure HIV. Also be clear that AIDS is caused by secondary infections once the immune system fails — not by HIV itself.
For TMV questions, AQA often asks you to explain the effect on the plant. Give the full chain: TMV damages chloroplasts → less photosynthesis → less glucose → stunted growth / reduced yield.
revisionCardBullets: Measles: spread by airborne droplets | symptoms: fever + red rash | can be fatal | prevented by MMR vaccine
Herd immunity: ~95% vaccination rate needed to stop measles spreading through a population
HIV: spread by sexual contact or exchange of body fluids (e.g. sharing needles)
HIV destroys white blood cells → immune system fails → AIDS
AIDS = immune system too damaged to fight infections — caused by secondary infections, not HIV directly
Antiretroviral drugs control HIV but do NOT cure it
TMV: plant pathogen — causes mosaic discolouration on leaves
TMV damages chloroplasts → less photosynthesis → less glucose → stunted growth
Viral disease controls: vaccination, antiviral drugs, hygiene, quarantine — NOT antibiotics
